name: Volvestre Hills Cycling Loop
name_original: Entre Lasserre et le village de Plagne
description: This moderate 30.3-mile road cycling route between Lasserre and Plagne offers challenging climbs and scenic alpine views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Montardit features a diverse landscape, encompassing the rolling Volvestre Hills and the flatter terrain of the Salat Plain. The region is characterized by a mix of open countryside and routes that ascend towards mountain passes like the Col de la Core. Road cyclists can expect varied elevations, from gentle gradients to sustained climbs. Best road cycling routes around Montardit The most popular road cycling route is Volvestre Hills Cycling Loop, a 30.3 miles (48.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 37 minutes to complete. This moderate route navigates the undulating terrain of the Volvestre Hills. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Core Pass – Seix loop from Lorp, a difficult 42.9 miles (69.2 km) path. This route includes significant elevation gain as it approaches the Core Pass. Local road cyclists also love the Col de la Core – Core Pass loop from Lorp-Sentaraille, a 51.8 miles (83.4 km) trail leading through mountain pass terrain, often completed in about 4 hours 30 minutes. Road cycling around Montardit is defined by rolling hills, expansive plains, and challenging mountain passes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Montardit are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 60 reviews. More than 2,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Montardit's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Montardit?
A: There are nearly 100 road cycling routes around Montardit, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. You'll find everything from easy rides through the plains to challenging ascents towards mountain passes.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Montardit?
A: Yes, Montardit offers several easy road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28838274" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Lizier Historic Village – Fields in the Salat Plain loop from Lorp-Sentaraille&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, providing a gentler ride through the flatter terrain of the Salat Plain.

Q: What kind of challenging road cycling routes can I find in the Montardit area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Montardit features routes with significant elevation gain, particularly those heading towards mountain passes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/36281934" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de la Core – Core Pass loop from Lorp-Sentaraille&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 51.8-mile (83.4 km) trail that includes substantial climbing.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Montardit?
A: Many of the road cycling routes in Montardit are designed as loops. A popular moderate circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e945170245/volvestre-hills-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Volvestre Hills Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which navigates the undulating terrain of the Volvestre Hills.

Q: What attractions can I see along the road cycling routes in Montardit?
A: While cycling around Montardit, you can encounter various points of interest. Consider a route that passes by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5780979" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Lizier Historic Village&lt;/a&gt;, known for its historical significance. You might also find sections of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2092634" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piémont Pyrénéen Greenway (V81)&lt;/a&gt;, which incorporates old railroad embankments.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Montardit?
A: The region around Montardit is generally pleasant for road cycling from spring through autumn. The varied landscape, from rolling hills to mountain passes, offers different experiences depending on the season, with summer being ideal for higher elevations and spring/autumn for milder rides.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that pass through towns or villages with amenities like cafes?
A: Yes, some routes will take you through or near local towns where you can find cafes and other amenities. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/29020366" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint Girons – Pont Vieux de Saint-Girons loop from Lorp-Sentaraille&lt;/a&gt; passes through Saint-Girons, a larger town where you can refuel.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Montardit?
A: The road cycling routes in Montardit are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 60 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse scenery, from the gentle plains to the challenging mountain ascents, and the well-maintained roads.

Q: Are there any routes that offer scenic viewpoints?
A: Routes that ascend towards the mountain passes, such as those involving the Col de la Core, naturally offer spectacular panoramic views of the surrounding Pyrenean foothills and valleys. The elevation gains on these routes are rewarded with expansive vistas.

Q: Can I find road cycling routes that are suitable for families with children?
A: While many routes involve some elevation, easier options like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28838274" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Lizier Historic Village – Fields in the Salat Plain loop from Lorp-Sentaraille&lt;/a&gt;, which covers flatter terrain, could be more suitable for families looking for a less strenuous ride. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile to ensure it matches your family's ability.

Q: Are there any less-known or 'hidden gem' road cycling routes in Montardit?
A: Beyond the most popular trails, Montardit's extensive network of nearly 100 routes means there are many quieter roads to discover. Exploring the smaller, local roads through the Volvestre Hills or the Salat Plain can reveal charming villages and peaceful countryside away from busier paths.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Montardit?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and elevation. Easy routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28838274" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Lizier Historic Village – Fields in the Salat Plain loop from Lorp-Sentaraille&lt;/a&gt;, can take around 1 hour 15 minutes. More challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38715268" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Core Pass – Seix loop from Lorp&lt;/a&gt;, can take over 3 hours 30 minutes, with some longer mountain pass loops extending to 4 hours 30 minutes or more.
tips: The old church was linked to the Cistercian abbey of Lumière-Dieu, in Comminges, destroyed during the Revolution. It is a small rural church built in the 17th century on foundations from the Romanesque period, and whose door is sheltered by an emban at the foot of the bell tower, itself pierced by two bays where the bells are located. Inside, the single nave is covered with a wooden coffered ceiling.
A charming little village. Small roads with little traffic to get there.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the road cycling route Between Lasserre and the village of Plagne?
faqs.answer: This road cycling route is approximately 48.7 km long and typically takes around 2 hours and 37 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Komoot classifies this route as 'moderate'. However, road cycling in the wider La Plagne region is known for its challenging terrain, featuring steep and sustained climbs with numerous switchbacks. While this specific route is moderate, be prepared for significant elevation changes and a good workout.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is predominantly on well-paved roads, typical for road cycling. However, some less main roads in the area might feature poorer tarmac or even unpaved sections that can turn into gravel. Expect challenging ascents and descents characteristic of mountain cycling.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle Between Lasserre and the village of Plagne?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the warmer months when the roads are clear of snow and ice. Given the alpine environment, late spring through early autumn typically offers the most favorable conditions, allowing you to fully enjoy the stunning mountain landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest to look out for?
faqs.answer: Yes, along the route, you can visit the Fabas market square and church. As you approach the destination, you'll also find the Church of Plagne.

faqs.question: What are the scenic highlights along the way?
faqs.answer: Cyclists are treated to 'nice views' towards the Beaufortain mountains and expansive vistas into the valley. The route often winds through forested areas and opens up to mountain pastures, offering varied and picturesque backdrops. The high-altitude environment provides stunning alpine landscapes throughout the ride.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking details for Lasserre are not provided, generally for road cycling routes in this region, you would look for public parking areas in or near the starting village. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling, which typically means dogs are not suitable companions, especially given the distance and potential traffic. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular cycling adventure.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay any entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road route between Lasserre and the village of Plagne.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly crosses or runs alongside several local paths, including sections of the Chemin de Bigot, Chemin de Mérous, Chemin de Lobit, Chemin de la Barrère, Chemin des Aubuts, Chemin des Chaumes, Chemin du Lavoir, Chemin de la Goutte des Joncs, Chemin de la Vignasse, Chemin du Lac, Rue Honoré Icard, Chemin de Ausseing à Montclar, Chemin des Mattes, Chemin du Sourbe, Rue du Sarrat de Nauch, Chemin de Pradet, Chemin de La Goutte, and Chemin de la Guinguette.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants along the route?
faqs.answer: While specific establishments are not listed directly on the route, both Lasserre and the village of Plagne, being populated areas, are likely to have options for refreshments or meals. It's always a good idea to carry snacks and water, especially on longer rides in mountainous terrain.
